title: Chapter Six: The Game
content: Eighty-nine thousand upvotes.

I stare at the number until it stops looking real. Until it becomes just shapes, just geometry on a screen. Eighty-nine thousand people have pressed their fingers against this story. Have absorbed it. Have carried it somewhere inside themselves.

The notifications are a river with no mouth. Comments, messages, awards. Someone has crossposted to three other subreddits. A moderator pinned it. A journalist has DM'd me — *would you be willing to talk on the record?*
I close the laptop.

Breakfast waits downstairs. I know this because I can smell it — toast, butter, something sweet that might be marmalade. The manor conducts scent through its hallways like a body conducts blood. Everything arrives at every room eventually.
I sit at the table. The twins are already there.
They watch me enter. I feel it — the synchronized turn of their heads, the identical angle of their chins. Not matching. Never quite matching. But close enough to make the back of my neck tighten.
"Sleep well?" Miles asks.
He butters his toast. The knife moves in a slow, deliberate arc. His eyes stay on me.
"Fine," I say.
Flora smiles. She cradles her teacup in both hands. Her fingers are very small around the porcelain.
"I heard you typing," she says. "Late. Were you writing a diary?"
The question lands like a coin dropped on a marble floor. Bright. Unmistakable.
"I was writing," I say. "Not a diary."
"What, then?"
"Notes. For my dissertation."
"Oh." Flora sips her tea. "That sounds important."
Miles says nothing. He eats. But his attention has not left me — I can feel it, a low and steady pressure, like a hand pressed flat against a window.

I eat my toast. I do not look at my laptop, sitting closed on the sideboard. I do not think about the 89,000 people who now know about Bly Manor, about the lake, about the thing someone saw in the reflection.
I do not think about how the twins might feel if they knew.

But they already know. That is the thought I cannot shake. They heard me typing. They know I am writing about them. They are not stupid — they are something far more dangerous than stupid. They are precise.

The morning passes. Flora reads. Miles practices piano — something Baroque, something with too many notes, something that climbs and climbs and never resolves. I sit in the library and pretend to read and instead listen to the music winding through the walls like ivy.
I am not reading. I am planning.
Evening drops over Bly like a held breath finally released.

I come back from dinner — tinned soup, crackers, an apple I couldn't finish — and find it. A teacup on the floor outside my door. Steam curls from its surface. A small saucer beneath it, a folded napkin beside it.
Chamomile.

I kneel. The smell reaches me before my fingers touch the cup. Chamomile — sweet, grassy, familiar. And under it, something else. Faint. Sharp. A chemical undertone, like bleach left too long in a plastic container. Like something that should not be in tea.
I do not drink it.
I stand. I look up and down the hallway. Empty. The sconces throw their jittery light on the wallpaper. Somewhere below, a door closes.

I go into my room. I find the thermos I brought from London — still in my bag, still clean. I come back. I pour the tea into the thermos, slowly, carefully. Not a drop spilled. I seal it. I set it on the desk beside my laptop.
Evidence.

The word arrives in my mind clean and cold, like a key turning in a lock. I have evidence now. Not just a feeling. Not just the crawl of unease across my skin. A substance. A sample. Something I can hold up to the light and say: *this was given to me. This was left outside my door. Smell it. Tell me I imagined that.*
My hands are steady. This surprises me. I expected them to shake.
I did not drink the tea. This is the important part. This is the part I will remember later, when I am less certain of everything. I did not drink it. I saved it instead.
I cap the thermos. I put it in my bag, wrapped in a sock, padded against breakage. I brush my teeth. I wash my face. I open my laptop and set the recording to start.

The webcam program runs in the background. It captures the room in grainy gray-green night vision. The timestamp ticks in the corner. I have four hours of battery if I don't turn on the screen. I close the lid halfway. I leave it on the desk, angled toward the bed.
I lie down. I close my eyes.
I do not sleep. Not for a long time. I lie in the dark and listen to the house settle, to the pipes contract, to the far-off sound a large building makes when it is thinking. When it is waiting.
When I finally sleep, it is not by choice.
Morning.
I reach for the laptop before I reach for my glasses. The battery is dead — the recording stopped sometime in the night. I plug it in. I wait. I open the file.
Fourteen hours of footage. The timestamp reads from 11:02 PM to 9:47 AM.
That is wrong.
I set the recording to start at 11 PM. I slept around midnight. The battery should have died around 3 AM. Four hours. Maybe five. Not fourteen.
I scroll through the timeline. The footage plays normally until 2:47 AM. I see myself in the bed, motionless, the blanket pulled to my chin. The desk chair. The dark window. The door, slightly ajar.
At 2:47 AM, the image cuts to static.
Not a glitch. Not a brief hiccup. Full static. White noise. The timestamp keeps running — 2:48, 2:49, 2:50 — but the image is gone. Just salt. Just noise.
The static lasts for three hours and eleven minutes.
At 5:58 AM, the footage returns. The room is the same. I am in the bed, in the same position. The door is still ajar. The desk chair has not moved.
But something is different.
I watch the footage from 5:58 AM. I watch it three times before I see it.
The door.
When the static begins at 2:47 AM, the door is ajar by four inches. I can see the gap. I can see the dark hallway beyond it.
When the footage returns at 5:58 AM, the door is ajar by two inches.
Someone closed it slightly. Someone was in the room during the three hours of static. Or someone was at the door before the static and after it, and the static was not an accident but an erasure.
I go back. I watch the moments before the cut.
2:46 AM. The room. My sleeping form. The door.
2:46:30. A shadow moves in the gap of the door. A shape. Pale.

2:46:45. The shape resolves. A face. Flora's face. She is standing in my doorway, looking in. Her eyes catch some faint light — the laptop's recording light, maybe, or the moon through the thin curtain. Her expression is not curious. Not afraid. Not curious is the wrong word.
Her expression is intent.
She is watching me sleep. Standing perfectly still in the doorway. Watching.
At 2:47 AM, static.
I sit with this for a long time. The laptop keys are cold under my fingers. The room is cold. I am cold in a way that has nothing to do with temperature.
Flora stood in my doorway and watched me sleep. Then the footage disappeared for three hours. Then the door was closer to shut.
I do not know what happened in those three hours. I do not know if she came inside. I do not know if she touched my things, read my notes, found my thermos of saved tea.
I check the thermos. Still there. Still sealed. Still in my bag.
Evidence.
I close the laptop. I breathe.
I find Miles in the garden.

He is sitting on the stone bench near the fountain — the fountain that doesn't work, that hasn't worked in what looks like decades. He has a book open in his lap, but he is not reading. He is looking at the tower.

The tower. I see it from where I stand — the east wing's roofline broken by the narrow stone column, the window at its top dark and small. It is the highest point of Bly Manor. It looks like a finger pointing at the sky, accusing it of something.
Miles hears me. He turns.
"Good morning," he says. His smile is the usual smile — polished, mechanical, the smile of a child who has been taught to smile by someone who did not understand what smiles were for.
"Good morning," I say. I sit on the bench beside him. The stone is cold through my jeans. "Miles, can I ask you something?"
"You're going to anyway."
"Tell me about the man who died."
The smile does not change. But something behind it does. A flicker, like a candle flame in a draft. His eyes lose their focus for a half-second, then sharpen.
"Peter," he says. "Peter Quint. He was our friend. He was very kind."
The words are well-rehearsed. Each one placed carefully, like stones in a wall.
"How did he die?"
"He fell."
"From the tower?"

The question lands wrong. I know it immediately. I have touched something I should not have touched. The crack in Miles's face is not a crack — it is a splintering, a fault line running through porcelain, spreading faster than control can contain it. His mouth opens. Closes. His jaw tightens. For one full second, I see something real behind the mask.
It is grief. It is rage. It is something older than either, something that has burrowed down into the root of him and lives there, coiled and patient.
Then the mask rebuilds. Brick by brick. The smile returns.
It is worse than the crack.

"How do you know about the tower?" he asks. And this time the smile is genuine. It reaches his eyes. His eyes are bright and warm and terrible. A child who knows a secret. A child who is proud of that secret. A child who will never, ever tell.
"I saw it from the lake," I say. "It's visible from the lake path."

His smile does not waver. He is deciding whether to believe me. I can see the calculation — not a child's calculation, not an adult's calculation, but something that belongs to neither. Something new. Something that grew here, in this house, in the soil of Bly, in the dark between walls.
"The tower is dangerous," he says. "You shouldn't go up there. The stairs are very old."
"I won't," I say.

"Good." He closes his book. He stands. He looks down at me, and for a moment the morning light catches his face at an angle that erases his age entirely. He could be eight. He could be forty. He could be something that has never had an age at all.
"We worry about you, Maeve," he says. "Flora and I. We want you to be safe here."
He walks away across the garden. His footsteps are soundless on the gravel.

I sit on the cold bench for a long time after. My chest is tight. My breath comes shallow. I am not afraid of what Miles said. I am afraid of how he said it. The genuine smile. The warmth. That is the most frightening thing I have seen at Bly. Not the dark. Not the silence. Not the chemical smell of tea.
The thing that looked like love.
The shift happens over the next day. Not all at once. A slow unwind.

Flora's sweetness acquires an edge. She brings me water at lunch and her hand lingers on the glass a moment too long, her fingers brushing mine. When I flinch, she smiles. Not the old smile — a new one, thinner, with something behind it I cannot name.

Miles stops performing entirely. The polite phrases, the careful small talk — gone. He eats in silence. He answers questions with single words. When I enter a room, he looks at me as if measuring me for something.
The mask has not slipped. The mask has been set aside. They are not trying anymore.
I do not know what this means.

I should be relieved. I should feel that the performance was the threat and its absence is safety. I do not. The performance was the wall. Now the wall is down, and I can see the shape behind it — large, patient, waiting.
The game has changed. We are no longer playing hide-and-seek. We are playing something else. Something with different rules. Rules I have not been told.

I keep my door locked. I keep the thermos in my bag. I check the laptop recording each morning and find only stillness, only sleep, only the empty room. Flora does not appear again on the footage. The static does not return.
This is not comforting. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. It means she is more careful now. It means she knows about the recording.
It means they both know.
Night.

I lie in bed and stare at the ceiling. The radiator clicks. A moth throws itself against the window. I have not taken my sleeping pills. I will not take them again. Not here. Not with a locked door between me and the world, and something on the other side of it that watches and waits and brings chamomile that smells like bleach.
I sleep anyway. The body betrays the mind eventually. Always.
Morning.
The sixth morning at Bly.
I wake to the moth still tapping. To gray light through the thin curtain. To the sound of my own breathing, rapid and shallow, as if I have been running in my sleep.
I sit up. I stretch. And I see it.
On my pillow.
A polaroid photograph.

The white border is slightly curled, the image small and square and saturated with that particular polaroid warmth — colors too bright, shadows too soft. The room is cold and the photograph is warm. It should not be here. Nothing should be here. I locked the door. I checked it twice.
I pick it up.

A woman stands at Bly Manor's front door. The door is recognizable — the arched frame, the iron handle, the crack running through the left panel. The woman is facing away from the camera. She is wearing a coat.
My coat.
The long dark wool coat I bought in Camden Market three years ago. The one hanging right now in my wardrobe. The one I wore the day I arrived.
I look at the woman's hair. Dark, shoulder-length, slightly curled at the ends. I look at her posture. The slight tilt of the head. The way one hand rests on the door frame.
It could be me. The photograph could be me, arriving at Bly for the first time, taken from behind by someone already inside.
But.

The coat is not only mine. My mother had one like it. I remember — a photograph in my grandmother's kitchen, my mother at the front door of some house, wearing a dark wool coat, her hair dark, her hand on the frame. I was told she left. I was told she was gone. I was never told where she went.
The woman in the polaroid could be me. She could be my mother. The resemblance is the point. The ambiguity is the point.
I turn the photograph over.
On the back, in blue ballpoint pen, child's handwriting:
**WELCOME HOME.**
The letters are round and careful. Flora's handwriting. I have seen it on grocery lists, on the labels she makes for the kitchen jars.
I hold the photograph in both hands. The paper is smooth and slightly warm, as if it has been clutched in a small fist. As if someone carried it here.

My chest aches. Not with fear. With something worse. With the shape of a feeling I did not know I still had — the wish, animal and ancient and completely without dignity, to be wanted somewhere. To be expected. To arrive at a door and have it open.
Welcome home.

The cruelty is not in the threat. The cruelty is in the tenderness. They know. Somehow — the photograph of my mother, the coat, the door — they know what would hurt me most. Not by frightening me. By finding the wound and pressing their small warm hands against it and whispering: *we see you. We have always seen you.*
I set the photograph on the nightstand, face down.
I will not show them that it worked.
I will not show them the tears.
I lock the door again. I check the window. I open my laptop. I start a new document. I type the date and the words:

*Evidence log, Day 6. Item 4: Polaroid photograph, left on pillow. Back reads "WELCOME HOME" in Flora's handwriting. Photograph shows woman in my coat at Bly front door. Possible depiction of my mother. Analysis pending.*
I save the document. I close the laptop.
The moth is still at the window. Tap. Tap. Tap.
I watch it. I do not let it in.
